title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: vignette demonstrates Partial Credit Model (PCM) Rasch analysis workflow using easyRaschBayes. analysis uses eRm::pcmdat2 dataset, small polytomous item response data set included eRm package. functions package work Bayesian brms model object fitted acat (adjacent categories) family, parameterises PCM. Dichotomous Rasch models can also fit using brms analyzed functions package. code example available . brief text explaining output interpretation vignette. extensive treatment Rasch analysis steps, please see easyRasch vignette.

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: eRm::pcmdat2 wide format item responses coded 0, 1, 2, …. brms acat family expects response categories starting 1, add 1 every response reshaping long format.
code: library(easyRaschBayes) library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble) library(ggplot2) df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  head(df_pcm) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   id    item  response #>   <chr> <chr>    <dbl> #> 1 1     I1           2 #> 2 1     I2           2 #> 3 1     I3           2 #> 4 1     I4           2 #> 5 2     I1           1 #> 6 2     I2           1

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: model fitted saved disk. code chunk shows fitting call (evaluated R CMD check). pre-fitted model stored fits/fit_pcm.rds loaded instead.
code: prior_pcm <- prior("normal(0, 5)", class = "Intercept") +   prior("normal(0, 3)", class = "sd", group = "id") fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data    = df_pcm,   family  = acat,   prior   = prior_pcm,   chains  = 4,   cores   = 4,   iter    = 2000 ) saveRDS(fit_pcm, "fits/fit_pcm.rds") fit_pcm <- readRDS("fits/fit_pcm.rds")

what: Item Fit: Infit and Outfit Statistics
title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: infit_statistic() computes posterior predictive infit outfit statistics item. Values near 1.0 indicate good fit; values substantially 1 suggest underfit (unexpected responses), values 1 suggest overfit (predictable). ndraws_use argument limits number posterior draws used, speeds computation exploration. final reporting, use draws (set ndraws_use = NULL omit ). infit_obs indicates observed conditional infit, can compared infit_rep, akin model expected value. Posterior predictive p-values (*_ppp) close 0.5 indicate observed statistic falls near centre posterior predictive distribution, implying good fit. Values near 0 1 warrant investigation.
code: fit_stats <- infit_statistic(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500)  # Posterior predictive p-values summarised per item fit_stats %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     infit_obs = round(mean(infit),3),     infit_rep = round(mean(infit_rep),3),     infit_ppp = round(mean(infit_rep > infit),3)   )

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: item_restscore_statistic() computes Goodman-Kruskal’s gamma item’s observed responses rest score (total score minus focal item). well-fitting Rasch model, gamma positive moderate; high values may indicate redundancy, low negative values suggest item relate well latent trait. output limited columns 1 5 output .
code: rest_stats <- item_restscore_statistic(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500)  rest_stats[,1:5] %>%    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3)))

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: plot_residual_pca() performs principal components analysis person-item residuals plots loadings first contrast together item locations uncertainty . Substantial loadings first contrast suggest multidimensionality. Items positive loadings cluster one end, negative loadings . observed largest eigenvalue smaller replicated, unidimensionality supported. ppp close 1.
code: pca <- plot_residual_pca(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500) pca$plot

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: q3_statistic() computes Yen’s Q3 statistic — correlation person-item residuals every item pair. conditioning latent trait, residuals uncorrelated; elevated Q3 values indicate local dependence (LD). primary metric ppp, close 1. output filtered ppp values 0.95. Pairs flagged locally dependent examined substantive overlap item content.
code: q3_stats <- q3_statistic(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500)  q3_stats %>%    filter(ppp > 0.95)

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: plot shows probability using response category y axis latent score x axis. crossover points, lines meet, item category threshold locations. Uncertainty shown shaded area around line.
code: plot_ipf(fit_pcm, theta_range = c(-6,5))

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: plot_targeting() produces Wright map (person–item targeting plot) showing distribution person locations alongside item threshold locations logit scale. Good targeting occurs person item distributions overlap substantially. person distribution systematically item thresholds, test may easy hard sample.
code: plot_targeting(fit_pcm)

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: RMUreliability() provides Bayesian reliability estimate via Relative Measurement Uncertainty (RMU, see Bignardi et al., 2025). requires matrix person location draws dimensions persons×drawspersons × draws. output point estimate lower/upper 95% highest density continuous intervals (HDCI). RMU values range 0 1, higher values indicating higher reliability, similarly traditional reliability metrics.
code: person_draws <- fit_pcm %>%   as_draws_df() %>%   as_tibble() %>%    select(starts_with("r_id")) %>%   t() rmu <- RMUreliability(person_draws) rmu

title: Bayesian Rasch Analysis Using brms
text: easyRaschBayes provides functions reproduce classic Rasch analysis features using Bayesian item response theory (IRT) models fitted brms. supports dichotomous Rasch models polytomous partial credit models, exposes full posterior distribution output.

title: Bayesian Rasch Analysis Using brms
text: Install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("pgmj/easyRaschBayes")

title: Bayesian Rasch Analysis Using brms
code: library(brms) library(easyRaschBayes)  # Fit a Bayesian Rasch model (dichotomous) fit <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = my_data,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4, cores = 4 )  # Item fit infit_statistic(fit)  # Person-item map plot_targeting(fit)  # Local dependence q3_statistic(fit)

text: use function, need provide matrix (input_draws) contains posterior draws parameter wish calculate reliability. function assumes rows input_draws represent subjects columns represent posterior draws. example apply function calculate mean score reliability using brms, see tutorial. example apply function go/go-task data using brms, see tutorial.

what: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
text: Tests differential item functioning (DIF) Bayesian Rasch-family models fitted brms comparing item parameters across subgroups defined exogenous variable. function fits DIF model includes group--item interactions summarizes posterior distribution DIF effects.

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
code: dif_statistic(   model,   group_var,   item_var = item,   person_var = id,   data = NULL,   dif_type = c("uniform", "non-uniform"),   prob = 0.95,   rope = 0.5,   refit = TRUE,   ... )

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
text: model fitted brmsfit object baseline (-DIF) model. group_var unquoted variable name identifying grouping variable DIF testing (e.g., gender). Must factor character variable exactly 2 levels current implementation. item_var unquoted variable name identifying item grouping variable. Default item. person_var unquoted variable name identifying person grouping variable. Default id. data optional data frame containing variables needed DIF model, including group variable. NULL (default), function attempts use model$data. Since baseline model formula typically include group variable, brms dropped stored model data. case, must supply original data frame . dif_type Character. polytomous ordinal models . "uniform" (default) tests uniform location shift per item via group:item fixed-effect interaction. "non-uniform" fits group-specific thresholds per item computes per-threshold DIF effects difference groups. Ignored dichotomous models. prob Numeric \((0, 1)\). Width credible intervals. Default 0.95. rope Numeric. Half-width Region Practical Equivalence (ROPE) around zero DIF effects, logit scale. Default 0.5, corresponding practically negligible DIF effect. Set 0 skip ROPE analysis. refit Logical. TRUE (default), DIF model fitted automatically updating baseline model via update, reuses compiled Stan code faster sampling. FALSE, DIF model formula returned (useful manual fitting custom settings). ... Additional arguments passed update.brmsfit refitting DIF model (e.g., cores, control).

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
text: list following elements: summary tibble one row per item (uniform DIF) per item × threshold (non-uniform DIF) containing: item, optionally threshold, dif_estimate (posterior mean), dif_lower, dif_upper (credible interval), dif_sd (posterior SD), pd (probability direction), rope_percentage (proportion inside ROPE), flag (classification). dif_draws matrix posterior draws DIF effects (draws × effects), analysis. dif_model fitted DIF brmsfit object (refit = TRUE), NULL. dif_formula brmsformula used DIF model. baseline_model original baseline model. plot ggplot forest plot DIF effects credible intervals ROPE.

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
text: polytomous models, two types DIF can tested: Uniform DIF (dif_type = "uniform", default) single location shift per item across groups, modelled group:item fixed-effect interaction. tests whether average item difficulty differs groups. Non-uniform / threshold-level DIF (dif_type = "non-uniform") item receives group-specific thresholds via thres(gr = interaction(item, group)). DIF effects computed difference threshold groups, revealing whether DIF affects specific response categories. function constructs DIF model adding group--item interaction baseline model: Dichotomous models (family = bernoulli()): baseline response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id) becomes response ~ 1 + group + (1 + group | item) + (1 | id), group slope varying item captures item-specific DIF. Polytomous uniform DIF (dif_type = "uniform"): baseline response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id) becomes response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + group:item + (1 | id). Polytomous non-uniform DIF (dif_type = "non-uniform"): baseline becomes response | thres(gr = item_group) ~ 1 + (1 | id), item_group = interaction(item, group). item × group combination gets thresholds. DIF effects differences group-specific thresholds item, computed draw--draw posterior. DIF effects summarized using: Probability Direction (pd) proportion posterior dominant side zero. Values > 0.975 indicate strong directional evidence. ROPE Region Practical Equivalence (Kruschke, 2018). > 95\ DIF effect practically negligible. > 95\ outside, effect practically significant. Credible Interval CI excludes zero, evidence DIF specified credibility level.

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Dichotomous Rasch with DIF testing ---  set.seed(123) df <- expand.grid(id = 1:200, item = paste0("I", 1:10)) %>%   mutate(     gender = rep(sample(c("M", "F"), 200, TRUE), each = 10),     theta  = rep(rnorm(200), each = 10),     delta  = rep(seq(-2, 2, length.out = 10), 200),     dif    = ifelse(item == "I3" & gender == "F", 1.0,              ifelse(item == "I7" & gender == "F", -0.8, 0)),     p      = plogis(theta - delta - dif),     response = rbinom(n(), 1, p)   )  fit_base <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4, cores = 4, iter = 2000 )  dif_result <- dif_statistic(   model     = fit_base,   group_var = gender,   data      = df )  dif_result$summary dif_result$plot  # --- Partial Credit Model: uniform DIF ---  df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   mutate(gender = sample(c("M", "F"), n(), TRUE)) %>%   pivot_longer(!c(id, gender),                names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data   = df_pcm,   family = acat,   chains = 4, cores = 4, iter = 2000 )  # Uniform DIF (default): one shift per item dif_uni <- dif_statistic(fit_pcm, group_var = gender, data = df_pcm) dif_uni$plot  # Non-uniform DIF: threshold-level effects dif_nu <- dif_statistic(fit_pcm, group_var = gender, data = df_pcm,                          dif_type = "non-uniform") dif_nu$summary dif_nu$plot } # }

title: Posterior Predictive Item Fit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — fit_statistic_pcm
text: function implements posterior predictive checking approach item fit described Bürkner (2020). procedure works follows: Draw posterior expected category probabilities via posterior_epred posterior predicted responses via posterior_predict. ordinal categorical models (3D array output posterior_epred), extract probability assigned observed response category replicated response category draw observation. code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Polytomous Rasch (Partial Credit Model) ---  # Prepare data in long format df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  # Fit a Partial Credit Model using the adjacent category family fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data    = df_pcm,   family  = acat,   chains  = 4,   cores   = 4,   iter    = 2000 )  # Categorical log-likelihood criterion (for polytomous models) ll_categorical <- function(y, p) log(p)  # Compute item fit statistics item_fit <- fit_statistic_pcm(   model      = fit_pcm,   criterion  = ll_categorical,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Summarise: posterior predictive p-values per item item_fit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   )  # Use ggplot2 to make a histogram library(ggplot2) item_fit %>%   ggplot(aes(crit_diff)) +   geom_histogram(aes(fill = ifelse(crit_diff > 0, "above","below"))) +   facet_wrap("item") +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "none")  # Compute person fit statistics person_fit <- fit_statistic_pcm(   model      = fit_pcm,   criterion  = ll_categorical,   group      = id,   ndraws_use = 500 ) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  # Prepare binary response data in long format df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  # Fit a dichotomous Rasch model fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  # Bernoulli log-likelihood criterion ll_bernoulli <- function(y, p) y * log(p) + (1 - y) * log(1 - p)  # Compute item fit statistics item_fit <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_rm,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Summarise: posterior predictive p-values per item item_fit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   )  # Use ggplot2 to make a histogram library(ggplot2) item_fit %>%   ggplot(aes(crit_diff)) +   geom_histogram(aes(fill = ifelse(crit_diff > 0, "above","below"))) +   facet_wrap("item") +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "none")  # Compute person fit statistics person_fit <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_rm,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  person_fit %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   )  # --- 1PL model with item-specific intercepts ---  # Alternative parameterisation with fixed item effects fit_1pl <- brm(   response ~ 0 + item + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  item_fit_1pl <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_1pl,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 500 )  item_fit_1pl %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   ) } # }

title: Posterior Predictive Infit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — infit_statistic
text: procedure adapts conditional infit/outfit statistics (Christensen et al., 2013; Kreiner & Christensen, 2011; Müller, 2020) Bayesian framework: posterior draw \(s\), category probabilities \(P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)\) obtained posterior_epred. conditional expected value variance observation computed : $$E^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c c \cdot P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)$$ $$Var^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c (c - E^{(s)}_{vi})^2 \cdot       P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)$$ Standardised squared residuals : $$Z^{2(s)}_{vi} = (X_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi})^2 / Var^{(s)}_{vi}$$ Outfit unweighted mean \(Z^{2}_{vi}\) across persons within item. Infit variance-weighted mean: $$Infit^{(s)}_i = \frac{\sum_v Var^{(s)}_{vi} \cdot       Z^{2(s)}_{vi}}{\sum_v Var^{(s)}_{vi}}$$ computations repeated replicated data \(Y^{rep}\) drawn via posterior_predict. perfect fit, infit outfit expected value 1. Values substantially 1 indicate underfit (much noise), values 1 indicate overfit (little variation, e.g., redundancy). Posterior predictive p-values near 0 1 indicate misfit.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Partial Credit Model (polytomous) ---  df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data   = df_pcm,   family = acat,   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  # Compute infit per item item_infit <- infit_statistic(   model      = fit_pcm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Summarise across draws item_infit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     infit_obs = mean(infit),     infit_rep = mean(infit_rep),     infit_ppp = mean(infit_rep > infit)   )  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  item_infit_rm <- infit_statistic(   model      = fit_rm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  item_infit_rm %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     infit_obs = mean(infit),     infit_rep = mean(infit_rep),     infit_ppp = mean(infit_rep > infit)   ) } # }

Compute standardized residuals observed data: $$z^{obs(s)}_{vi} = \frac{X_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi}}       {\sqrt{Var^{(s)}_{vi}}}$$ Generate replicated data \(Y^{rep(s)}\) posterior_predict compute standardized residuals: $$z^{rep(s)}_{vi} = \frac{Y^{rep(s)}_{vi} -       E^{(s)}_{vi}}{\sqrt{Var^{(s)}_{vi}}}$$ Reshape sets residuals person \(\times\) item matrices perform SVD . Extract first-contrast eigenvalue item loadings observed replicated SVDs. Compare eigenvalues across draws: posterior predictive p-value ppp = mean(eigenvalue_obs > eigenvalue_rep) tests whether observed residual structure stronger model produces assumptions. style = "density" style = "", draw-level (location, loading) pairs item used construct filled 2D kernel density contours via geom_density_2d_filled. lowest contour level (outside contours) set transparent white panel background shows . title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
text: procedure works follows posterior draw \(s\): Compute expected values \(E^{(s)}_{vi}\) category probabilities returned posterior_epred. ordinal models: \(E^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c c \cdot     P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)\). binary models: \(E^{(s)}_{vi} =     P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = 1)\). Compute observed residuals: \(d^{(s)}_{vi} = X_{vi} -     E^{(s)}_{vi}\). Compute replicated residuals: \(d^{rep(s)}_{vi} =     Y^{rep(s)}_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi}\), \(Y^{rep}\) drawn via posterior_predict. item pair \((, j)\), compute Q3 Pearson correlation residuals across persons responded items. Aggregate across draws obtain posterior means, quantiles, posterior predictive p-values. key advantage parametric bootstrapping reference distribution obtained directly posterior, automatically accounting parameter uncertainty negative bias inherent Q3 (depends test length person ability distribution).

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Partial Credit Model ---  df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data   = df_pcm,   family = acat,   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  # Q3 residual correlations q3_results <- q3_statistic(   model      = fit_pcm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  # Flag item pairs with ppp > 0.95 as locally dependent q3_results %>%   filter(ppp > 0.95) %>%   arrange(desc(q3_diff))  # Inspect 99% credible intervals for Q3 differences q3_results %>%   filter(q3_diff_q005 > 0)  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 4,   iter   = 2000 )  q3_rm <- q3_statistic(   model      = fit_rm,   item_var   = item,   person_var = id,   ndraws_use = 500 )  q3_rm %>%   filter(ppp > 0.95) } # }
